assumptions: The question concerns consolidated financial statements, not individual or separate financial statements.
The dividend is already recognised as an intragroup receivable, not merely a forecast intragroup distribution.
The context does not state whether the entities involved have different functional currencies; that is treated as a critical condition.
No investment entity fact pattern is stated.

approaches.label: General intragroup hedge prohibition in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, IFRS 9 generally allows only items with external parties to be designated as hedged items. An intragroup dividend receivable therefore does not qualify by default.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not document the hedge in consolidation unless a specific IFRS 9 exception applies.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 restricts hedged items in consolidated financial statements to exposures with parties external to the reporting entity, subject to stated exceptions.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. The designated risk can be the FX risk component if it is separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
conditions: the recognised intragroup dividend receivable is a monetary item
the intragroup monetary item creates FX gains or losses that are not fully eliminated on consolidation
the entities involved have different functional currencies
the designated hedged risk is the foreign currency risk component and it is reliably measurable
formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements in IFRS 9 paragraph 6.4.1 are met
approaches.practical_implication: A hedge relationship may be documented for the FX risk of the receivable if the monetary-item exception and all hedge-accounting criteria are satisfied.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when the FX effects are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Paragraph 6.3.7 and B6.3.8–B6.3.11 allow designation of a specific risk component if it is separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation and documentation at inception, plus the hedge effectiveness criteria.
Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies fair value and cash flow hedge models for recognised assets or liabilities when the hedged exposure could affect profit or loss.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 separately allows certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged for FX risk if that risk affects consolidated profit or loss. That exception does not match a dividend already recognised as receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: If the exposure is already booked as a receivable, assess it under the intragroup monetary-item exception instead of the forecast-transaction exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction to qualify only when it is in another currency and the FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraphs B6.3.5–B6.3.6 explain that forecast intragroup transactions do not qualify if their FX risk does not affect consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge model
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of net investments in foreign operations, where the hedged item is an amount of net assets. An intragroup dividend receivable is not the same as the foreign operation's net assets.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not document the dividend receivable hedge as a net investment hedge unless the exposure being hedged is the qualifying net investment itself.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7–8 limit the Interpretation to hedges of net investments in foreign operations.
Paragraphs 10–12 define the hedged risk as FX differences between functional currencies and the hedged item as an amount of net assets.
Paragraph 14 requires the normal IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements to be satisfied for a net investment hedge.
